description: 'pelaku' is the degree of a health effect against stated criteria. Pain, loss of function, bodily danger, and daily burden can place the same condition differently because each uses another dimension. Severity also leaves urgency open: a mild condition may need prompt care, while a severe long-term condition may not have changed today.
articulatory_notes: Close the lips for unvoiced 'p' and release into true-mid front unrounded 'e'. Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for voiced lateral 'l', let air pass along its sides, and open into stressed central 'a'. Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release into high-back rounded 'u'.
usage_notes: 'pelaku' can refer to one degree or to severity as a dimension. The condition and criterion identify what was assessed; the source and affected person's report show whose account supports the judgement. 'kisome' (assess) names the comparison with that criterion.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Naming the dimension prevents a severity judgement from becoming a ranking of whose experience or care matters.

semantic_domains.physical: degree of bodily or health effect
